1.synthesis: 'Then the LORD said to Noah, "Go into the ark, you and all your household, for I have seen that you are righteous before me in this generation. Take with you seven pairs of all clean animals, the male and his mate, and a pair of the animals that are not clean … For in seven days I will send rain on the earth forty days and forty nights"' (vv.1–4). The divine command to enter the ark is the fulfillment of the covenant-promise of Genesis 6:18, and it adds a detail absent from the ch6 command: seven pairs of clean animals, one pair of unclean. The additional clean animals anticipate the sacrifice of ch8 — Noah will need reserves to offer burnt offerings without depleting species. The seven-day countdown begins: 'And Noah did all that the LORD had commanded him' (v.5), the second comprehensive obedience-statement in as many chapters. Noah was 600 years old when the flood began (v.6).
1.historical_context: The clean/unclean animal distinction here predates the Mosaic law by centuries, establishing that the ritual categories of Leviticus are not Levitical innovations but primordial categories with deep roots (cf. Leviticus 11; Deuteronomy 14). The practical motivation for seven pairs of clean animals was twofold: surplus for sacrifice and more rapid reproductive replacement of food-animal populations after the flood. The seven-pair instruction also builds in margin against disease and loss during the year aboard. The forty-day rain duration is a structuring number in the flood narrative: Genesis 7:12 gives 40 days of rain; this will recur in Moses's 40 days on Sinai and Israel's 40 years in the wilderness as the standard wilderness-testing period. YHWH's declaration 'I have found you righteous before me' (v.1) is the divine forensic verdict confirming what the narrator reported in ch6 — the judgment is both observation from outside and declaration from above.
1.literary_structure: The section is structured around two divine speeches (vv.1–4) and their corresponding human obedience (v.5) and editorial summary (vv.6–10). The seven-day countdown creates narrative tension: the flood is coming in a week, and every detail of the ark-loading matters. The clean/unclean distinction (v.2) introduces ritual order into the crisis context — even in judgment, the categories that will govern Israel's worship are operative. The obedience-formula of v.5 ('Noah did all that the LORD commanded him') directly echoes v.22 of ch6, creating an obedience refrain that frames the flood preparation.
1.theological_application: Gill on 'I have seen that you are righteous before me' (v.1): 'He was not only righteous in his own sight, and in the sight of others, but in the sight of God, who knows the hearts of men, and tries the reins; he could look into his heart, and saw the root of righteousness there, the principle of grace, from whence his righteous actions flowed.' Matthew Henry on the clean animals: 'Seven of every clean beast, that is, seven of a sort, for Noah had all the animals, clean and unclean, brought to him … The clean were more in number than the unclean, for God loves to show mercy, and as those that were most useable to man in his state of innocency were the clean … so still more of those were preserved.' The pre-flood knowledge of clean and unclean animals underscores that the Mosaic law codifies a moral and ritual order that was not invented at Sinai but is rooted in creation.
1.christology: The divine command 'go into the ark' (v.1) is addressed to one household amid a condemned world — precisely the pattern of election, where the many perish and the few are preserved through the representative covenant-head. Noah's righteousness before God (v.1) does not earn his survival — the covenant was established before (6:18) — but it characterizes the one through whom the remnant will pass. The forty days of rain (v.4) will become the forty-day pattern of the NT: Jesus's forty days of wilderness testing (Matthew 4:2) — where the second Adam resists what the first Adam failed — runs parallel to the forty-day judgment on the world that the first Adam's sin brought. The one who endures the testing opens the covenant to all who are in him.

11.synthesis: 'In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, on the seventeenth day of the month, on that day all the fountains of the great deep burst forth, and the windows of the heavens were opened. And rain fell upon the earth forty days and forty nights' (vv.11–12). The flood's onset is dated with unusual precision: year, month, day — the narrative marks the event as historical in its specificity. The source of the flood is twofold: the fountains of the deep (subterranean waters) and the windows of heaven (the waters above the firmament, from the creation's Day 2 separation). This is the un-creation of Genesis 1: the ordered separation of waters above and below is reversed, and the primordial sea reclaims what was land. 'And all flesh died that moved on the earth, birds, livestock, beasts, all swarming creatures that swarm on the earth, and all mankind … only Noah was left, and those who were with him in the ark' (vv.21–23).
11.historical_context: The twofold source of the flood — 'fountains of the great deep' (maʿyĕnôt tĕhôm rabbāh) and 'windows/floodgates of the heavens' (ʾărubbōt haššāmayim) — directly inverts the Day 2 cosmogony: the waters above the firmament (Genesis 1:7) and the waters below are reunited. The flood is not a large rainstorm but a cosmological reversal, the undoing of the ordered creation. The Epic of Gilgamesh (Tablet XI) and the Atra-hasis epic record similar flood traditions: boat-builder, divine warning, animals, birds released, sacrifice after. The similarities demonstrate shared cultural memory; the differences — monotheism vs. divine conflict, moral cause of flood vs. divine overpopulation — demonstrate the Genesis account's theological distinctiveness. The comprehensive death-catalog of vv.21–22 ('all flesh … in whose nostrils was the breath of life') echoes Genesis 2:7 (YHWH breathing life into Adam): what was breathed in is now extinguished.
11.literary_structure: The section moves from the dated onset (vv.11–12) through the boarding sequence (vv.13–16: Noah, his sons, their wives, the animals) to the flood's duration and extent (vv.17–24). The boarding sequence of vv.13–16 is carefully structured: the closing of the door by YHWH himself (v.16: 'the LORD shut him in') is the narrative's most theologically loaded action — the ark is sealed by divine hand, and what is outside cannot enter. The death-catalog of vv.21–22 uses the same comprehensive categories as the creation-narrative's blessing: birds, livestock, beasts, swarming creatures, humankind. The formula 'only Noah was left (wayyiššāʾer)' is the remnant-formula, recalling the Sethite line's preservation through the catalog of death.
11.theological_application: Gill on v.16: 'And the Lord shut him in — in the ark, to preserve him from the waters; he was the door of the ark, both shut it and held it, so that no water could enter, and there was no need of Noah's care in this matter.' Matthew Henry on the scope of the flood: 'Let us observe throughout this account the utter destruction of sinners; and wonder at the patience of God; that when men sin so long and so grievously, he should bear so long, and wait to be gracious. But observe also the certain salvation of the saints in their troubles.' On 'the LORD shut him in': 'God shut the door on the outside, and thus kept it fast. Note, God himself is the protector of his people, and their defence is of him. It is he that shuts the door upon them, so that the destroying angel cannot enter.' The providential shutting of the door is an image of sovereign preservation: Noah is not safe because of his own vigilance but because God sealed the entrance.
11.christology: The LORD's shutting of Noah in the ark (v.16) is the most direct divine action of preservation in the flood narrative. The closed door that excludes the waters is the same imagery Jesus uses in both directions: 'I am the door. If anyone enters by me, he will be saved' (John 10:9) and 'the door was shut' in the parable of the ten virgins (Matthew 25:10). The one through whom entry is given is also the one who closes the door to the judgment. The universal death of v.21 — 'all flesh died … birds, livestock, beasts, all swarming creatures … and all mankind' — is the shadow of what Romans 5:12 articulates: 'sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin, and so death spread to all men.' Noah's ark anticipates Christ as the locus of the remnant's preservation through universal death.
